refinement_sections.content: The problem is about analyzing survey data from a health organization to see if wellness programs help reduce employee stress. But I need to understand what the data looks like and how to test if the programs work.
The survey data comes from different departments, but we don't know how stress levels vary between them. I need to find out if there are different starting points for stress in each department.
I think I need to choose a statistical test to compare the stress levels before and after the wellness programs. Maybe a t-test or ANOVA could work, but I need to check if the data fits these tests.
I need to assume that the data is normally distributed and that the variances are similar across the groups. If these assumptions are not met, I might need to use a different test.

I will set up a null hypothesis that says the wellness programs have no effect on stress levels, and an alternative hypothesis that says they do have an effect. Then I will collect the data, run the test, and see what the results say.

After I conduct the test, I will look at the p-value to decide if I reject the null hypothesis or not. If the p-value is less than 0.05, I might conclude that the wellness programs help reduce stress.
